schema_notes: Third Rashidun caliph. Early convert from the Umayyad clan. Twice migrated to Abyssinia with his wife Ruqayya (the Prophet's daughter). As caliph, stayed almost entirely in Medina; his most consequential act was standardising the Quran into the codex used ever since.
sources: Ibn Ishaq, Sirat Rasul Allah (Guillaume tr.)
Wilferd Madelung, The Succession to Muhammad

journey.event: Born to the Banu Umayya clan of Quraysh, one of the wealthiest families of Mecca. His father Affan was a prominent merchant.
Became a successful cloth merchant like his father. Known for gentleness, modesty, and generosity.
Among the very earliest converts to Islam, brought in by Abu Bakr. Soon after, married the Prophet's daughter Ruqayya.
Faced severe persecution from his own Umayyad kin. The Prophet instructed the persecuted Muslims to migrate to Abyssinia under the Christian Negus.
First migration to Abyssinia with his wife Ruqayya — among the very first group of emigrants. The Prophet said of him, 'Uthman is the first to emigrate with his family since Lot'.
Returned briefly to Mecca on false news that the Quraysh had accepted Islam.
Migrated to Abyssinia a second time as persecution resumed. Remained there until the general return.
Made the Hijra to Medina with Ruqayya.

Did not participate in the Battle of Badr: Ruqayya was gravely ill in Medina and the Prophet ordered him to stay and care for her. She died the day the Muslims returned victorious from Badr. The Prophet assigned him a share of the spoils as if he had fought.
Married another daughter of the Prophet, Umm Kulthum, after Ruqayya's death — earning his title Dhu al-Nurayn, 'Possessor of the Two Lights'.
Present at Uhud.
Fought at the Battle of the Trench.

Sent by the Prophet from Hudaybiyyah into Mecca as his envoy to the Quraysh because of his Umayyad connections. Rumours of his death in Mecca triggered the Pledge of the Tree (Bay'at al-Ridwan) in which the Muslims swore to fight to avenge him.
Participated in the conquest of Mecca.
Fought at Hunayn.
Financed one third of the Tabuk expedition personally — 900 camels, 100 horses, and a large sum of gold. The Prophet said that day, 'Nothing Uthman does after this day will harm him'.
Present at the Farewell Pilgrimage.
Senior companion and advisor during the caliphates of Abu Bakr and Umar.
Chosen as the third caliph by the six-man shura Umar had appointed from his deathbed. Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf offered the caliphate first to Ali and then to Uthman; Uthman accepted the conditions.

Ruled almost entirely from Medina. The conquests continued under his direction: the fleet victory at the Battle of the Masts (655) against Byzantium, campaigns across North Africa, and the consolidation of Persia.

Ordered the standardisation of the Quran. Regional variations in recitation were threatening the community's unity; he had an authoritative codex compiled under Zayd ibn Thabit and sent copies to the main garrison cities, ordering all other copies burned. The Uthmanic codex became the textual basis of every Quran ever since.

The last years of his caliphate were troubled by complaints from the provincial garrisons — particularly Kufa, Basra, and Egypt — over the appointment of Umayyad kinsmen as governors. Rebels gathered in Medina demanding his abdication.

Besieged in his own house in Medina by rebels from Egypt and Iraq. Refused to abdicate or to allow defenders to shed blood on his behalf. Killed on 17 June 656 while reading the Quran; drops of his blood are said to have fallen on the verse 'God will suffice you against them' (2:137). Buried quietly in Medina. His death opened the First Fitna.
